Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Jean-Paul Sartre

1905 – 1980

Consciousness as a nothingness that is free: imagination, the ego, being and nothingness.

Sartre never wrote a “philosophy of mind” in the contemporary analytic sense. What he gives us is more interesting and less modular: a phenomenological psychology of consciousness, imagination, emotion, and the ego; a phenomenological ontology of consciousness, embodiment, action, and other people; and a later existential-psychoanalytic and historical anthropology. Extracted as a philosophy of mind, Sartre’s view is this:

The mind is not a soul-substance, not a private inner container, not a theater of representations, not an ego that owns experiences, and not a hidden unconscious machinery. Consciousness is an event of world-directed disclosure: it is always consciousness of something, and it is always tacitly self-aware without becoming an object to itself. The ego, personality, psyché, character, and “inner life” are not the foundation of consciousness; they are constituted objects, stabilized in reflection, conduct, social recognition, and biography. Human mindedness is embodied, situated, temporal, affective, imaginative, practical, and exposed to others. Sartre is therefore neither a Cartesian substance dualist nor a reductive physicalist. He is best read as an anti-substantialist, anti-internalist, existential-phenomenological theorist of consciousness and personhood.

1. Corpus and problem: where Sartre’s “mind theory” is found

The relevant corpus begins before Being and Nothingness. The early works are not marginal: The Transcendence of the Ego (1936), The Imagination (1936), Sketch for a Theory of the Emotions (1939), and The Imaginary (1940) are the laboratory in which Sartre develops his psychology of consciousness. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes this early period as a Husserlian but distinctive analysis of intentional acts, and notes that Sartre discovered phenomenology in Berlin in 1933, wrote The Transcendence of the Ego, published his work on imagination in 1936, his emotion theory two years later, and Being and Nothingness in 1943.

A useful Lusophone reception makes the same point with unusual clarity: Sartre’s relevance to psychology is usually divided into three domains, namely phenomenological psychology, existential psychoanalysis, and possible clinical inspiration; the same article stresses that Sartre’s early investigations are especially concerned with a phenomenological psychology of intentional life, including emotion and imagination. This matters because Sartre’s philosophy of mind is not a late metaphysical add-on. It is already operative in his analyses of images, emotions, selfhood, and reflection.

The decisive methodological borrowing is Husserlian intentionality, but Sartre radicalizes it. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes Sartre’s early discovery of phenomenology as a way to overcome the realism/idealism opposition and to “extract philosophy” from concrete objects as they are seen and touched; Sartre then uses intentionality against the very idea of consciousness as an inner realm. This anti-interiority is the hinge of everything that follows.

2. Sartre’s basic anti-internalism: consciousness has no “inside”

Sartre’s first thesis is that consciousness is not a container. To say that consciousness is intentional is not merely to say that mental states have representational content. For Sartre, intentionality means that consciousness is outside itself, at the object. When I perceive a tree, I am not first aware of an internal tree-representation and then infer or construct the external tree. I am directed toward the tree itself. The IEP summarizes Sartre’s development of Husserl by saying that consciousness is transparent, has no “inside,” and is a “fleeing” toward the world.

This is why Sartre rejects what he calls the “illusion of immanence”: the tendency to treat images, feelings, sensations, and meanings as little things inside consciousness. The object of consciousness is not normally inside consciousness. Pierre’s repulsiveness, for example, is not first a private feeling inside me; Pierre appears as repulsive. Sartre’s early analysis of affective experience already denies that reflection can move validly from a lived attraction or repulsion to a stable inner state such as “I hate Pierre.”

This yields a direct-realist or anti-representational tendency, though not a naïve realism. Perception, imagination, emotion, and desire are not internal episodes plus external interpretation. They are different ways in which a world appears and matters. A Sartrean philosophy of mind therefore begins not with “mental contents,” but with world-disclosing comportments.

3. Consciousness: positional, non-positional, and reflective

Sartre’s core distinction is between positional consciousness and non-positional self-consciousness. Positional consciousness is consciousness of an object: the cup, the melody, the problem, the absent friend, the threatening dog. Non-positional self-consciousness is the tacit self-presence of the act itself. When I am absorbed in reading, I am conscious of the text; I am also tacitly conscious of reading, but not as an object. If I explicitly say “I am reading,” reflection has entered and the earlier act has been objectified.

This is not a higher-order theory in which one mental state monitors another. Sartre’s point is precisely that the self-awareness of consciousness is built into the conscious act itself. The SEP entry on phenomenological approaches to self-consciousness states the general phenomenological thesis: prereflective self-consciousness is present whenever I live through an experience, and Sartre is cited as holding that this self-consciousness is not an added quality but the very mode of being of consciousness.

Spanish-language analytic reception has developed this point in connection with self-representational theories of consciousness. Eduardo Ortiz Medina’s Scielo article argues that Sartrean prereflective consciousness is composed of positional consciousness of an object and non-positional self-consciousness; crucially, these are not two separate mental states, but one internally differentiated conscious state. The same article uses Sartre’s pain example to show the point: pain does not first exist unconsciously and then receive a later beam of consciousness; pain exists as consciousness of pain.

That last point is central for extracting a philosophy of mind. Sartre is not saying that every conscious experience is reflectively known, conceptually articulated, or introspectively mastered. He is saying that a conscious episode cannot be unconscious in its own mode of existence. The distinction is between consciousness and knowledge. Consciousness can be non-thematic, implicit, lived, and opaque to later interpretation, but it is not hidden behind itself.

4. The ego is not the owner of consciousness

The most radical early doctrine is that the ego is not inside consciousness. Sartre rejects Husserl’s transcendental ego, Kantian apperceptive ego, and Cartesian ego-substance. In The Transcendence of the Ego, the ego is not the subject that produces consciousness; it is an object constituted in reflection. The SEP summarizes Sartre’s claim: pre-reflective consciousness and reflective consciousness must not be confused; the ego is the object of reflective consciousness, and Sartre says it is “outside, in the world,” like the ego of another.

This is a decisive anti-Cartesian move. Descartes begins with “I think”; Sartre begins with consciousness of the world and only later explains how an “I” appears. When I run for the bus, solve a problem, or flee danger, there need be no ego-object present. The act is conscious, but it is not necessarily experienced as “my act” under a thematic “I.” Reflection later appropriates it: “I was running,” “I was afraid,” “I solved it.” The IEP explains Sartre’s view by saying that the ego is produced by consciousness, not prior to it, and that pre-reflective consciousness contains no “I” at that level.

This gives us four layers that are often conflated:

Layer Sartrean status
Consciousness / for-itself World-directed, tacitly self-aware activity
Prereflective self-awareness Non-objectifying self-presence of the act
Ego / “I” / “Me” Transcendent object constituted by reflection
Person / character / biography A historical, social, embodied totalization of conduct

The result is not a denial of selfhood, but a relocation of it. The ego is real enough as an object in the world, but it is not the metaphysical source of consciousness. Sartre’s “self” is not a hidden owner but a project, a practical unity, and often a socially mediated object.

5. The psyché: why psychology studies constituted objects

Sartre distinguishes consciousness from the psyché. Consciousness is not a thing; the psyché is the quasi-objective field of states, qualities, dispositions, and ego-unities that reflection and social life stabilize. Love, hate, jealousy, cowardice, generosity, “my personality,” and “my character” belong to this psychic field. They are not simply fictitious, but they are not ontologically primitive.

The Portuguese clinical-psychological article notes Sartre’s distinction between immediate emotions such as repulsion or anger and reflective “states” such as hatred: one can be conscious of repulsion toward Pierre without being certain that one hates him. This is exactly Sartre’s point: the state “hate” exceeds the immediate episode. It is a reflective synthesis across time, conduct, expectation, and interpretation.

A French discussion of Sartre’s development from ontology to history describes “psychic temporality” as distinct from original temporality; psychic life is associated with impure or constituting reflection, which objectifies states and qualities as if they belonged to a self to be realized. This is important because it prevents a crude reading of Sartre as saying “there is no personality.” Rather, personality is a constituted formation, not an inner substance.

For psychology, the implication is severe. Psychology goes wrong when it treats psychic facts as natural things governed by causal laws analogous to physical laws. It becomes adequate only when it interprets them as meaningful structures of world-involvement. Sartrean psychology is not anti-empirical, but it is anti-naturalist in the sense that human conduct cannot be understood merely as the resultant of forces.

6. Ontological background: in-itself, for-itself, and nothingness

Being and Nothingness gives Sartre’s mature ontology of mind. He distinguishes being-in-itself (en-soi) from being-for-itself (pour-soi). Being-in-itself is the dense, self-identical being of things. Being-for-itself is consciousness: not a substance, but a lack, distance, negation, and transcendence. The SEP summarizes this as a distinction between consciousness, which negates and transcends, and the “pure plenitude” of objects.

This is why Sartre famously says that the for-itself is “what it is not and is not what it is.” The point is not verbal paradox. A chair is what it is. A person is never merely what they are factually: tired, poor, French, a waiter, ashamed, ill, gifted, traumatized. A person lives those facts under possibilities, refusals, projects, and meanings. Consciousness introduces absence, possibility, and negation into being.

Spanish-language scholarship on Sartre’s consciousness as freedom captures this well: consciousness is described as a spontaneous or prereflective emergence, a non-substantial absolute, and a structure whose freedom must later be understood as embodied and situated rather than as a merely abstract purity. That “non-substantial absolute” phrase is useful: Sartre is not making consciousness into an immaterial thing. He is saying that consciousness is not thing-like at all.

This ontology is also a theory of mental intentionality. To be minded is to be able to encounter what is not: the missing friend, the possible revolution, the unreal image, the future self, the value not yet realized, the meaning of what has been done. Absence and possibility are not secondary intellectual judgments; they are built into the structure of consciousness.

7. Perception: directness, profiles, and practical world-disclosure

Sartre’s theory of perception is less often discussed than his theories of imagination and emotion, but it is foundational. Perception is not the possession of internal sensory data. It is a direct orientation toward transcendent objects. The object is never exhausted by the present profile, but it is given as more than what is currently seen. This is why perception is realist in orientation but phenomenological in method.

If I perceive a cube, I see one side while intending the cube as having other sides. If I perceive Pierre as threatening, I do not perceive a neutral body plus add an internal emotion; Pierre appears under a meaningful aspect. If I look for Pierre in the café and he is absent, that absence is a feature of the situation as lived, not merely a private judgment inside my head.

Perception is also practical. The world is disclosed as a field of obstacles, instruments, paths, risks, and solicitations. Sartre’s later account of action makes this explicit: motives and ends cannot be separated from the embodied context of the actor, and freedom is realized in projection and action, not in bare mental intention. Perception, in Sartre, is therefore closer to embodied affordance-perception than to passive sensation.

8. Imagination: the irrealizing function of consciousness

Sartre’s theory of imagination is one of his major contributions to philosophy of mind. He rejects the idea that imagining is having a picture in the mind. The imagined object is not an inner image-object. It is an intentional object posited as absent, nonexistent, elsewhere, or fictional. The IEP states that Sartre’s account “does away with representations” in the traditional internal-picture sense and treats imagination as a directed act toward the imagined object.

The SEP describes The Imaginary as a rigorous phenomenological psychology of the intentional relation to the unreal, centered on the “irrealizing” function of consciousness. In imagination, consciousness “nihilates” the real in a specific way: it posits an object as not present in the actual world. This is not mere fantasy in the pejorative sense. It is a structural capacity of consciousness.

This theory has several consequences.

First, imagination is not a degraded perception. Perception gives an existing object as inexhaustible; imagination gives its object as absent or irreal. Second, the image depends on an analogon—a photograph, drawing, actor’s body, words on a page, bodily impression, or other support—but the imagined object is not identical with that support. Third, imagination is tied to freedom. The SEP notes that Sartre links imagination to the ability to “surpass the real,” to step back from the world and constitute it as a world.

A Sartrean philosophy of mind therefore treats imagination as central, not peripheral. The power to think what is absent, possible, fictional, or otherwise is a fundamental structure of consciousness and action. It is also continuous with art, literature, planning, desire, and self-projection.

9. Emotion: not inner feeling, but magical transformation of the world

Sartre’s theory of emotion is equally anti-internalist. Emotion is not primarily an inner feeling caused by bodily disturbance. Nor is it a mere passive state that happens to us. It is a meaningful mode of apprehending and transforming the world. The IEP summarizes Sartre’s Sketch for a Theory of the Emotions as replacing the traditional passivity of emotion with the subject’s active participation: emotion is a spontaneous grasp of a situation as magically transformed.

Spanish commentary in Reflexiones Marginales is especially useful here. It emphasizes that, for Sartre, emotion is not describable in purely bodily terms, nor is it a disorder of the soul or a psychic imbalance; it is an immediate, non-reflective consciousness of the world, a way of transforming our being-in-the-world. The same analysis stresses that emotion remains object-directed: fear is not primarily attention to my trembling but to the threatening object; emotion persists as long as the object remains present, even if present as absent, imagined, or longed for.

The classic Sartrean examples are fainting before danger, weeping, rage, or the “sour grapes” structure. When instrumental action is blocked, consciousness changes the world’s practical meaning by changing its own bodily comportment. If I cannot remove the danger, I may faint; if I cannot obtain the desired object, I may transform it into something contemptible. This is “magical” not because Sartre believes in magic, but because emotion treats symbolic or bodily transformation as if it changed the practical structure of the world.

The body is indispensable here. A purely intellectual reinterpretation is not yet emotion. The Spanish article notes that without bodily changes, intellectual apprehension, and an act such as evasion or flight, Sartrean emotion disappears as emotion. Yet emotion is not voluntary in the ordinary sense. Sartre distinguishes spontaneity from deliberate will: a spontaneous consciousness can be active without being a reflective decision. The IEP makes this distinction explicit in relation to emotion and imagination.

The philosophy-of-mind extraction is powerful: affect is not a private quale added to cognition. Affect is a world-disclosive, embodied, action-oriented transformation of salience, possibility, and demand.

10. Desire, will, motivation, and action

Sartre’s theory of agency is often caricatured as “we can choose anything.” That is not his mature view. Sartrean freedom is not freedom as power, success, or arbitrary caprice. It is the structure by which facts become motives only within a projected field of ends. The SEP stresses that Sartre is not claiming we are free to achieve anything or project anything whatsoever; the for-itself is always in a factical situation, and motives and ends depend on how the given is negated and interpreted.

A factual state does not mechanically determine its meaning. Poverty, fatigue, illness, class position, a bodily condition, or a traumatic past can be crushingly real. But for Sartre, such facts become motives only through a project: revolution, resignation, endurance, shame, revenge, self-pity, solidarity, escape. This is why he says even suffering is not automatically a motive; it becomes one in relation to a possible transformation.

Will is not the origin of agency. Reflective willing often comes after a more basic prereflective orientation has already been established. This point appears already in Existentialism is a Humanism, where Sartre says what we usually call wishing or willing is often a manifestation of a prior, more spontaneous decision. In philosophy-of-mind terms, reflective volition is not the executive center of the mind. It is one mode of a deeper projective structure.

11. Temporality and memory: the mind is future-oriented

Sartrean consciousness is temporal through and through. It is never a static now containing mental contents. It is a temporalizing movement: retaining a past, projecting a future, and interpreting the present as a situation. The for-itself is not identical with its past because it is also its possibilities; but it is not free of its past because the past is its facticity.

The SEP notes that, in Being and Nothingness, we are not reducible to our past or objective attributes because we are projects, and projects are intrinsically future-oriented. French scholarship similarly emphasizes Sartre’s distinction between original temporality and psychic temporality: the first belongs to the temporalizing for-itself; the second is the temporality of the reflected psyché, the self understood as a sequence of states, qualities, and realizations.

Memory is therefore not merely retrieval of stored mental objects. Remembering is a present way of taking up a past in light of a project. Sartre’s later existential psychoanalysis radicalizes this point: the past has weight, but its significance is not causally fixed once and for all. It is taken up, reinterpreted, hidden, dramatized, repeated, or overcome within a life-project.

12. The body: Sartre’s alternative to soul/body dualism

Sartre is often accused of being a philosopher of disembodied consciousness. That reading is too simple. There is a real tension in Sartre: his language of consciousness as nothingness can sound disincarnate, but his ontology makes the body a permanent structure of being-in-the-world. French phenomenological reception already saw the body as indispensable: De Waelhens writes that if human existence is being-in-the-world, phenomenology must explain how I am my body, because it is through my body that I am present to and inserted in the world; this makes a duality between my subjectivity and the way I exist my body “impensable.”

Sartre’s body theory has three main dimensions.

First, there is the body-for-itself: the body I live, not the body I inspect. I do not primarily know my body as an anatomical object; I exist it as my point of view, my capacity, my fatigue, my reach, my vulnerability, my orientation. The body is not an object added to consciousness; it is the factical condition under which consciousness has a world.

Second, there is the body-for-others: my body as seen, judged, desired, racialized, sexualized, medically examined, or socially positioned by others. Shame is the paradigmatic experience: under the look, I live my body as an object for another.

Third, there is the body-object-for-me: the body known through medicine, physiology, anatomy, mirrors, photographs, and third-person observation. This is legitimate but derivative. A French analysis of Sartre’s body chapter identifies the body as facticity, the body-object of physiology, and the body as the “organ of the situation,” even saying that for the for-itself, existing and being situated are one.

A 2023 Brazilian article on “body and psyche” captures the interpretive upshot: Sartre’s body concept is already present in the early psychological writings and becomes clearer in Being and Nothingness, where the body challenges the classical opposition between corporeality, consciousness, and psyché.

So Sartre’s answer to the mind-body problem is not interactionism between two substances. Consciousness does not “use” the body as a soul uses an instrument; consciousness exists bodily. Yet Sartre is not a physicalist. The lived body is not reducible to the objective body of physiology, even though physiology is a valid third-person science of the body as object.

13. Soul and incorporeal mind: Sartre’s negative answer

Sartre has no positive doctrine of an immortal soul or incorporeal mind. His view is almost exactly designed to prevent such a doctrine. Consciousness is not a substance; the ego is not inside consciousness; the body is not an external vehicle; the person is a situated project; and there is no divine essence of man.

His atheistic existentialism is relevant here. In Existentialism is a Humanism, Sartre says that atheistic existentialism declares that if God does not exist, there is at least one being whose existence comes before its essence: man, who first exists and defines himself afterward. He then denies a fixed human nature grounded in God. This undercuts the classical theological idea that the human person is defined by a pre-given soul essence.

The most direct body/soul point appears in the body chapter of Being and Nothingness. A Spanish essay quoting and discussing Sartre highlights the claim that the body is not a contingent addition to the soul, but a permanent condition of consciousness as project. The word “soul” here functions polemically: Sartre invokes it to reject the thought that consciousness could be what it is without embodiment.

What about other minds as “souls”? Sartre sometimes uses inherited vocabulary such as âme, but he does not mean a separable immaterial substance. In the encounter with another, the other’s “soul” is not given as a private object inside a body; rather, the other is encountered through the look as a presence that exceeds my knowledge. A French article on the look notes Sartre’s formula that the other’s soul is not given in person to mine; the body indicates it without delivering it. That is not a Cartesian soul theory. It is a phenomenology of the other’s transcendence.

The best concise conclusion is this: Sartre rejects both the materialist reduction of consciousness to a thing and the spiritualist elevation of consciousness into a soul-thing. Consciousness is not matter, but neither is it an incorporeal substance. It is no-thing: a nihilation of being, lived bodily, temporally, socially, and practically.

14. Other minds: the look, shame, and social cognition

Sartre’s account of other minds is one of his most important contributions to philosophy of mind. He rejects the argument from analogy: I do not first observe another body, infer that it is like mine, and then infer that it contains another mind. That model presupposes the separation it is supposed to overcome. The SEP states that Sartre, like other phenomenologists, regards inferential reasoning by analogy as inadequate for establishing the existence of others or their mental states.

His alternative is the look. In the famous keyhole scene, I am absorbed in what I am doing. Then I hear footsteps. Suddenly I experience myself as seen. Shame is not an inference; it is an immediate transformation of my being. I become an object for another subject. The SEP describes this as an involuntary apprehension of oneself as an object in another’s eyes, without inferential theorizing.

This is a philosophy of social mind. The other is not primarily a mind hidden behind a body. The other is a freedom that decenters my world. My body, gestures, projects, and identity become exposed to an interpretation I do not control. In this sense, the mind is constitutively social: not because consciousness is a social construction all the way down, but because selfhood, shame, pride, role, gendered embodiment, racialized embodiment, and personal identity are transformed by being-for-others.

A University of Liège article usefully notes that Sartre’s account of the look strains the early dichotomy between consciousness and object: the other appears as “subject,” even though Sartre’s official ontology has only in-itself and for-itself as basic regions. This is one of the internal pressures in Sartre’s philosophy of mind. The other is neither a mere object nor simply my own consciousness duplicated. Sartre’s system has to stretch to accommodate social mindedness.

15. Bad faith: self-deception without a hidden unconscious

Bad faith is Sartre’s alternative to a large part of Freudian metapsychology. The problem is self-deception: how can one both know and not know something? Freud explains this by introducing unconscious wishes, censorship, repression, and psychic agencies. Sartre argues that this splits the subject and reproduces the very paradox it was meant to solve.

The SEP summarizes Sartre’s critique: Freud’s models split the subject into conscious, preconscious, unconscious, id, ego, and superego in order to explain how there can be a liar and a lied-to within one consciousness; Sartre accuses this model of reifying the structure and transferring the paradox to the censor, which must both know and not know what it represses.

Sartre’s own solution depends on the distinction between consciousness and knowledge. Bad faith is conscious, but not necessarily known in clear propositional form. I can live a meaning, flee it, perform it, disavow it, and organize my conduct around it without reflectively admitting it. The café waiter is not simply mistaken; he is attempting to be his role as if he were a thing, while also relying on the freedom that allows him to perform it.

Bad faith is therefore not an unconscious mechanism but a lived strategy of ambiguity. Human beings are both facticity and transcendence. Bad faith absolutizes one side against the other: “I am nothing but this role, wound, identity, diagnosis, class, past,” or “none of these facts bind me at all.” Both are evasions. A Sartrean philosophy of mind treats self-deception as a practical, affective, embodied, and socially reinforced way of living meaning, not as a hidden mental compartment.

16. Existential psychoanalysis: depth without the unconscious

Sartre does not merely reject Freud; he proposes a rival psychoanalysis. Existential psychoanalysis seeks the fundamental project: the organizing way a person chooses, interprets, and totalizes their life. This project is not an unconscious cause. It is lived, expressed, and enacted everywhere, but not necessarily known. The SEP states the paradox clearly: Sartre’s psychoanalysis is directed toward consciousness rather than an unconscious psyche, and Sartre holds that psychic acts are coextensive with consciousness.

This is not superficial psychology. Sartre agrees that psychic life is opaque and that we need a method for interpreting psychic facts. What he denies is that natural-scientific causal laws can explain the meaning of human conduct. The SEP emphasizes that Sartre rejects deterministic explanations in which the present is merely the causal result of the past; instead, past events receive meaning in relation to the subject’s present project.

The key sentence for reconstruction is this: the fundamental project may be fully experienced and wholly conscious without being known. That is Sartre’s replacement for the unconscious. It preserves depth, opacity, interpretation, and biographical unity, but refuses a hidden psychic region.

The Portuguese article on Sartre and psychology makes the same methodological distinction: existential psychoanalysis is a response to Freudian psychoanalysis, but it rejects mechanistic, biologistic, and naturalizing approaches in favor of understanding human beings as freedom in situation. It also notes that a full Sartrean clinic was not actually developed by Sartre; later clinical uses are inspired extensions, not completed Sartrean doctrine.

A modern reconstruction should be careful here. Sartre’s rejection of “the unconscious” is not best read as a denial that the brain performs subpersonal, nonconscious processing. Contemporary cognitive science makes that denial implausible. Sartre’s target is the idea of unconscious psychic meanings functioning like hidden conscious acts. A defensible Sartrean view can allow nonconscious neural and bodily processes as conditions of experience while denying that they are already meaningful psychic acts in the first-person sense.

17. Later Sartre: from consciousness to praxis, history, and social mediation

Sartre’s later work complicates the early picture. He does not simply abandon freedom, but he increasingly embeds it in history, class, scarcity, family, institutions, and material conditions. The SEP notes that after Being and Nothingness, Sartre refines existential psychoanalysis through literary-biographical studies of Baudelaire, Genet, Flaubert, and others, warning against deterministic interpretations while making his analyses more sensitive to concrete constraints.

In Search for a Method and Critique of Dialectical Reason, Sartre develops the progressive-regressive method: one moves regressively from the individual life to historical and social conditions, then progressively back to the individual’s singular way of surpassing those conditions. The same SEP section says Sartre’s later method aims to show what a person makes of what society has made of them.

For philosophy of mind, the later concept of the practico-inert is crucial. The SEP describes the practico-inert as social and historical sedimentation: the activity of others sustained and diverted by inorganic inertia. Social objects, institutions, queues, markets, property, racism, bureaucracy, and scarcity organize behavior and even produce serial feelings and serial thoughts.

The later Sartre therefore pushes his philosophy of mind toward social externalism. Mental life is not merely in the head, not merely in individual consciousness, and not merely in dyadic shame before the other. It is mediated by material systems, historical sedimentation, and collective structures that shape the field of possible projects.

18. Reconstructed Sartrean philosophy of mind: core theses

A comprehensive Sartrean philosophy of mind can be extracted as the following set of theses.

First, consciousness is intentional. Every consciousness is consciousness of something. This does not mean every mental state contains a representation; it means consciousness exists by being directed beyond itself.

Second, consciousness is prereflectively self-aware. It does not need a second act, ego, or inner observer in order to be conscious. The “for-me-ness” of experience is intrinsic to experience.

Third, consciousness is non-substantial. It is not a thing, not a soul, not a mental stuff, and not an ego-substance. Sartre’s “nothingness” should be read as anti-reification: consciousness is a gap, distance, or nihilation within being, not a ghostly object.

Fourth, the ego is constituted, not constituting. The ego is a reflective, worldly object. It organizes experience retrospectively and socially, but it is not the owner hidden inside experience.

Fifth, the psyché is real but derivative. Psychological states, traits, and personalities are constituted unities of conduct, affect, temporality, and reflection. They can be studied, but not as inner natural objects.

Sixth, perception is direct world-involvement. Perception is not inner sensation plus inference; it is practical disclosure of a meaningful world.

Seventh, imagination is irrealizing negation. To imagine is to posit an object as absent, nonexistent, elsewhere, or fictional. This is fundamental to freedom and possibility.

Eighth, emotion is embodied world-transformation. Emotion is not a private inner coloring; it is a bodily, non-reflective, magical reorganization of the world when ordinary instrumental action is blocked or transformed.

Ninth, motivation is project-dependent. Facts become motives only in a field of ends. Human action is neither causally determined by facts nor arbitrarily chosen from nowhere.

Tenth, mindedness is embodied. The body is not an instrument used by a soul. It is the lived condition of having a world.

Eleventh, mindedness is social. The other is not inferred as a hidden mind but encountered through the look, shame, language, roles, and institutions.

Twelfth, depth psychology does not require an unconscious psyche. Sartre replaces unconscious mechanisms with lived but not necessarily known projects, bad faith, and existential interpretation.

Thirteenth, the mind is historical. Later Sartre embeds consciousness in praxis, scarcity, class, family, and the practico-inert. Human subjectivity is singular, but never socially unmediated.

19. Where Sartre fits in contemporary philosophy of mind

Sartre is not a substance dualist. He rejects the soul/body picture and denies that consciousness is an incorporeal thing. He is not a reductive physicalist either, because consciousness is not an objective process describable from the third-person standpoint alone. He is not a classical representationalist, because he rejects inner representations as the basic furniture of mind. He is not a behaviorist, because lived meaning, prereflective awareness, and first-person disclosure are irreducible. He is not a functionalist in the standard computational sense, because psychological meaning is not exhausted by causal role.

His closest contemporary relatives are phenomenological intentionalism, embodied cognition, enactivism, existential psychiatry, hermeneutic psychology, and some forms of externalism. The SEP’s entry on phenomenological self-consciousness notes that contemporary phenomenological analyses of prereflective self-consciousness connect with embodiment, intersubjectivity, ecological psychology, and enactive perception. The direct link to contemporary philosophy of mind is also explicit in current scholarship: Pre-reflective Consciousness: Sartre and Contemporary Philosophy of Mind is a major edited collection devoted to precisely this bridge.

The Spanish self-representational reading is especially interesting but should be treated cautiously. It argues that Sartre can be interpreted as offering a self-representational theory of consciousness, because prereflective consciousness represents itself in a non-objectifying way. That is a fruitful analytic translation, but Sartre’s own vocabulary is not representationalist in the usual internalist sense. “Self-intimation” is closer than “self-representation” if representation suggests an inner object.

20. Main tensions and criticisms

The first tension is transparency. Sartre says consciousness is translucent or transparent to itself, but he also says psychic life is opaque and that existential psychoanalysis is needed. The solution is to distinguish prereflective consciousness from reflective self-knowledge. Experience is self-present, but its meaning as part of a life can be obscure.

The second tension is embodiment. Sartre rejects soul/body dualism, but his ontology of in-itself and for-itself can sound like a new dualism. Merleau-Ponty and later critics often accuse Sartre of retaining a too-sharp split between consciousness and being. Francophone debate recognizes this: a Persée discussion of Sartre and Merleau-Ponty notes criticism of Sartre’s supposed transparency and insufficient incarnation, while also warning against conflating consciousness, ego, and subject too quickly. The fair assessment is that Sartre is anti-Cartesian about the ego and soul, but still preserves a strong ontological duality between consciousness and thinghood.

The third tension is the unconscious. Sartre’s critique of Freud is powerful against homuncular versions of censorship, but it is too sweeping if read as a denial of all nonconscious mental processing. A revised Sartrean view should distinguish subpersonal neurocognitive processes from personal-level psychic meanings.

The fourth tension is social conflict. Sartre’s theory of the look often makes relations with others appear fundamentally conflictual. Later Sartre’s theory of groups, praxis, and the practico-inert gives a richer social ontology, but it never fully removes the early drama of objectification.

The fifth tension is normativity. Sartre gives a profound account of bad faith but only an incomplete account of authenticity and ethics in Being and Nothingness. His later ethics, politics, and biographies extend the view, but there is no single finished Sartrean moral psychology.

21. Final synthesis

Sartre’s philosophy of mind can be stated in one rigorous formula:

A mind is not an inner substance but an embodied, situated, prereflectively self-aware, world-directed activity of nihilation and projection, whose ego and psyché are constituted through reflection, action, temporality, social exposure, and history.

This formula explains the main domains.

Perception is world-disclosure. Imagination is irrealization. Emotion is magical embodied transformation. Desire is lack and project. Will is reflective and derivative. The ego is constituted. The psyché is an objectified unity of lived acts. The body is not an instrument of the soul but the lived condition of consciousness. The other mind is not inferred but encountered through exposure to the look. The unconscious, as a hidden psychic region, is rejected; depth is relocated into lived but not fully known projects. The soul, as incorporeal substance, has no place in the system.

The result is a philosophy of mind that is still unusually fertile: anti-Cartesian without being materialist, anti-reductionist without being spiritualist, phenomenological without being merely introspective, and psychological without treating human beings as mechanisms.

PreviousMartin Heidegger NextMaurice Merleau-Ponty

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
